searchTerms: center of the cosmos - norse mythology
name: Yggdrasil
longDescription: Center of the Cosmos - Norse Mythology The World Tree - Axis of All Nine Realms
shortDescription: Center of the Cosmos - Norse Mythology The World Tree - Axis of All Nine Realms
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance: Center of the Cosmos - Norse Mythology The World Tree - Axis of All Nine Realms.
icon:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 64 64">
  <circle cx="32" cy="32" r="28.8" fill="#8b7fff" opacity="0.2"/>
  <path d="M 16 48 L 32 16 L 48 48 Z" fill="none" stroke="#8b7fff" stroke-width="3"/>

geography: A cosmic ash tree of immense size; roots extend to nine different realms (Asgard, Midgard, Muspelheim, etc.); trunk is so vast it supports multiple worlds; three roots reach down to wells (Aesir's well, Mimir's well, Niflheim's spring); eagle perches on top
relatedEvents: Ragnarok - tree shakes and trembles as cosmos ends
Rebirth of new world from Yggdrasil's branches
Continuous battle between squirrel Ratatosk and eagle
Warriors climbing up and down the tree realms
Norns weaving fate at the base

keyMyths.title: Odin's Sacrifice for Knowledge
keyMyths.description: To gain wisdom and understand the runes, Odin hung himself on Yggdrasil for nine days and nights, pierced by his own spear. This act symbolizes the ultimate sacrifice for knowledge and the connection between the divine and mortal realms.

keyMyths.title: The Well of Urd
keyMyths.description: Located at the base of Yggdrasil, the Well of Urd is tended by the Norns, who shape the fates of gods and men. They water the tree with water and white clay from the well, ensuring its continued life and the preservation of cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: The Nidhoggr's Gnawing
keyMyths.description: At the roots of Yggdrasil dwells the dragon Nidhoggr, constantly gnawing at the tree's roots, representing the forces of chaos and decay that threaten the cosmic order. This constant battle between creation and destruction is a key theme in Norse cosmology.

keyMyths.title: Ratatoskr, the Squirrel Messenger
keyMyths.description: Ratatoskr is a squirrel that runs up and down Yggdrasil, carrying messages between Nidhoggr at the roots and the eagle at the top. The squirrel's malicious gossip stirs up conflict and maintains the tension within the cosmic structure.

keyMyths.title: Ragnarok and the Burning of Yggdrasil
keyMyths.description: During Ragnarok, the final battle, Yggdrasil is prophesied to shake and groan, signifying the destruction of the world. Though the world burns, some accounts suggest Yggdrasil survives, hinting at a cycle of destruction and rebirth.

sources.name: The Poetic Edda
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, primarily preserved in the Icelandic manuscript Codex Regius. It contains many of the key mythological stories.
sources.title: The Poetic Edda

sources.name: The Prose Edda by Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: An Old Norse textbook written in Iceland in the early 13th century. Its purpose was to preserve the understanding of Skaldic poetry, and to present and interpret many of the myths and legends of Norse mythology.
sources.title: The Prose Edda by Snorri Sturluson

sources.name: Hávamál
sources.description: A section within the Poetic Edda presenting a collection of Old Norse wisdom literature, offering advice on living.
sources.title: Hávamál

sources.name: Grímnismál
sources.description: A poem within the Poetic Edda in which Odin, disguised as Grimnir, reveals cosmological knowledge.
sources.title: Grímnismál

sources.name: Völuspá
sources.description: The first and arguably most important poem of the Poetic Edda. It narrates the history of the world from creation to Ragnarok.
sources.title: Völuspá
